codeSnapshot.label: log_loss.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.metrics import log_loss

# Binary log-loss (cross-entropy)
# L = -(1/n) Σ [yᵢ·log(p̂ᵢ) + (1-yᵢ)·log(1-p̂ᵢ)]
y_true = np.array([1, 1, 0, 0, 1])
y_prob = np.array([0.9, 0.7, 0.2, 0.3, 0.6])  # predicted probabilities

# Manual computation
eps = 1e-15  # numerical stability: clip probabilities away from 0 and 1
y_prob_clipped = np.clip(y_prob, eps, 1 - eps)
loss = -np.mean(y_true * np.log(y_prob_clipped) + (1 - y_true) * np.log(1 - y_prob_clipped))
print(f'Log-loss (manual): {loss:.4f}')
print(f'Log-loss (sklearn): {log_loss(y_true, y_prob):.4f}')
# → Log-loss ≈ 0.211 (lower is better; 0 = perfect, ln(2)≈0.693 = random)

# Effect of confidence on log-loss
# Correct prediction with high confidence: low log-loss
# Correct prediction with low confidence: higher log-loss
# Wrong prediction with high confidence: very high log-loss (catastrophic)
cases = [
    (1, 0.99, 'Correct + confident'),       # loss ≈ 0.01
    (1, 0.60, 'Correct + uncertain'),        # loss ≈ 0.51
    (1, 0.10, 'Incorrect + confident'),      # loss ≈ 2.30 (catastrophic)
    (1, 0.50, 'Completely uncertain'),        # loss = ln(2) ≈ 0.693
]
for y, p, label in cases:
    l = -(y * np.log(p) + (1-y) * np.log(1-p))
    print(f'{label}: {l:.3f}')

# Multi-class log-loss
# L = -(1/n) Σᵢ Σⱼ yᵢⱼ · log(p̂ᵢⱼ)   where yᵢⱼ ∈ {0,1} (one-hot)
y_true_mc = [0, 1, 2, 1]  # class indices
y_prob_mc = np.array([
    [0.8, 0.1, 0.1],   # predict class 0 (correct)
    [0.2, 0.7, 0.1],   # predict class 1 (correct)
    [0.1, 0.2, 0.7],   # predict class 2 (correct)
    [0.3, 0.5, 0.2],   # predict class 1 (correct, uncertain)
])
mc_loss = log_loss(y_true_mc, y_prob_mc)
print(f'Multi-class log-loss: {mc_loss:.4f}')

eli3: Log-loss (also called cross-entropy loss) measures how good your model's probability predictions are — not just whether they're right or wrong, but how confident they are. Saying '90% chance of rain' when it rains is better than saying '60% chance,' even though both are correct.

The key insight: log-loss punishes confident wrong answers much more than uncertain wrong answers. Saying '99% chance of class A' when the answer is class B gives a huge loss. This encourages models to output well-calibrated probabilities — not just the most likely class.

A perfect model has log-loss=0. A completely random model (always saying 50/50 for binary) has log-loss=ln(2)≈0.693. If your log-loss is higher than 0.693, your model is actively making things worse by outputting biased probabilities.

whenWhy.title: Evaluating calibration of probabilistic classifiers
whenWhy.body: Log-loss is a proper scoring rule — it is minimised by reporting true probabilities (not overconfident or underconfident). It rewards calibrated models: a model that says '70%' should be right 70% of the time for that claim to be optimal. Unlike accuracy (which only checks the argmax) or AUC (which only checks ranking), log-loss penalises miscalibration directly. Use log-loss when downstream decisions depend on probability estimates (medical diagnosis probability estimates drive treatment protocols; fraud scoring drives investigation thresholds).

whenWhy.title: Standard metric for classification benchmarks
whenWhy.body: Log-loss is the primary metric for Kaggle classification competitions and NLP/ML benchmarks where probability estimates matter. Binary log-loss = binary cross-entropy (the standard training loss for logistic regression and neural network classifiers). Evaluating with log-loss on a held-out set checks both discriminative ability and calibration — a model can have good AUC but poor log-loss if it discriminates well but produces poorly calibrated probabilities (e.g., outputs all near 0.5).

whenWhy.title: Log-loss as negative log-likelihood
whenWhy.body: Binary log-loss = negative average log-likelihood of a Bernoulli model: L = -E[log p(y|x,θ)]. Minimising log-loss is equivalent to maximum likelihood estimation under a Bernoulli (logistic regression) model. Multi-class log-loss = negative average log-likelihood under a categorical (softmax) model. This probabilistic interpretation makes log-loss the natural evaluation metric when the model outputs probabilities — it directly measures how surprised the model is by the true labels.

deepDiveIntro: Binary log-loss averages, over all samples, the negative log probability the model assigned to the correct label. For the multi-class case the same idea extends: sum the negative log probability of the true class for each example. Log-loss is a proper scoring rule — its expected value is uniquely minimized when the model reports the true probabilities. Any systematic over- or under-confidence increases the expected loss. [1]

Because the logarithm of zero is negative infinity, predicted probabilities must be kept away from exactly zero or one. Sklearn clips them automatically to a small range. The loss grows without bound as a model becomes confidently wrong, which is why confident mistakes are so heavily penalized. Conceptually, log-loss measures how many bits are needed to encode the true labels given the model's predictions. [1]

deepDive.heading: Proper Scoring Rules and Calibration
deepDive.body: Log-loss is a proper scoring rule: its expected value is minimized exactly when the model reports true probabilities, and any deviation from truth increases expected loss. A model can rank examples correctly (high AUC) while still producing poorly calibrated probabilities (high log-loss). Platt scaling and temperature scaling are post-hoc methods that improve calibration — and therefore log-loss — without changing the ranking. [1]

deepDive.heading: Log-loss for Confident Mistakes
deepDive.body: When the model assigns high probability to the correct class, log-loss is near zero. When the model assigns high probability to the wrong class — say, 99% confidence on an incorrect prediction — the loss grows very large. This heavy penalty for confident mistakes is what distinguishes log-loss from accuracy, which only cares whether the top-ranked class is correct. Models that overfit often become overconfident on training data, and that overconfidence shows up as elevated log-loss on the test set. [1]

deepDive.heading: Relationship to Cross-Entropy and KL Divergence
deepDive.body: Log-loss is identical to cross-entropy between the true label distribution and the model's predicted distribution. Minimizing cross-entropy is equivalent to minimizing KL divergence from the true distribution because the true distribution's entropy is a constant. Training a neural network with cross-entropy loss therefore maximizes the likelihood of the observed labels under the model — it is maximum likelihood estimation in a categorical family. [1]

youtubeLabel: Log Loss and Cross-Entropy — Classification Metrics
takeaways: L=−(1/n)Σ[y·log(p̂)+(1−y)·log(1−p̂)] — negative log-likelihood of Bernoulli model. [1]
Proper scoring rule: minimised by reporting true probabilities — rewards calibration. [1]
Confident wrong answers are catastrophically penalised: −log(0.01)≈4.6. [1]
Random model (p=0.5 always): log-loss=ln(2)≈0.693 — use as baseline. [1]
Equal to cross-entropy; minimising = minimising KL(true||model). [1]

edgeCases.q: How do you handle the log-loss of a model that outputs exactly 0 or 1?
edgeCases.a: log(0) = -∞, so a prediction of p=0 for the true class gives infinite log-loss. In practice: (1) sklearn clips predictions to [1e-15, 1-1e-15]. (2) Add label smoothing: replace hard 0/1 targets with ε/K and 1-ε+ε/K — prevents the model from being penalised infinitely for mispredictions. (3) Temperature scaling: after training, divide logits by T>1 to soften predictions. (4) Use Brier score (MSE of probabilities) instead of log-loss for heavy-tailed evaluation — Brier score is bounded in [0,1] and less sensitive to extreme probabilities. The numerical clipping approach is standard for evaluation; label smoothing and temperature scaling are for training and post-hoc calibration.

edgeCases.q: When does optimizing log-loss during training diverge from AUC on the test set?
edgeCases.a: Log-loss and AUC can diverge when: (1) Calibration vs discrimination: a model can rank examples correctly (high AUC) but assign poorly calibrated probabilities (high log-loss). Platt scaling improves calibration (log-loss) without changing AUC. (2) Distribution shift: the model's probability estimates are well-calibrated for the training distribution but not the test distribution. AUC (ranking) degrades less quickly than log-loss under class proportion shift because AUC is invariant to class imbalance. (3) Label noise: noisy labels increase minimum achievable log-loss (irreducible entropy) while AUC can still be high if the ranking is correct. In practice: track both — AUC for discrimination and log-loss for calibration.

sources.label: Good (1952) — Rational Decisions, Journal of the Royal Statistical Society
